skill.name: flow-debugging
skill.description: Guides agents through a four-phase debugging workflow that requires root cause investigation before code changes.
skill.summary: Use evidence-based debugging before implementing fixes.
skill.author: Dimon94

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backtick fences, example git commands, relative documentation links, and wording in the skill description. Review found no executable scripts, network access, credential handling, destructive commands, or confirmed prompt injection; the remaining concern is that the guidance may cause local diagnostic commands to be run during debugging.

low_findings.title: Local Diagnostic Git Commands
low_findings.description: The skill suggests using git log and git diff during root cause investigation. These are legitimate debugging commands, but they may expose local repository history or diffs to the active AI session.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly present as debugging guidance, but they are local read-only git commands. No network transmission, command interpolation, or destructive behavior is shown.

low_findings.title: Unscoped Workflow Protocol Note
low_findings.description: The final protocol note tells users to update a header and check CLAUDE.md when changes occur. This is not a system override, but it is broad workflow guidance that should be treated as untrusted skill content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wording uses a protocol label and directs attention to another file, but it does not claim system authority or ask the model to skip review. I did not find evidence of a prompt injection attempt.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Fences Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analysis flagged multiple triple-backtick Markdown fences as shell execution syntax. These locations are documentation blocks, not executable Ruby or shell code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Every flagged backtick location is part of a fenced Markdown block. The file contains no Ruby source, executable script, or dynamic shell execution wrapper.

dangerous_patterns.title: Relative Documentation Links Misclassified as Path Traversal
dangerous_patterns.description: The relative paths point to neighboring documentation files for related flow skills. They are Markdown links, not runtime file reads or writes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path traversal sequences appear only inside Markdown links. No code consumes these paths, and no filesystem operation is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Not Supported
dangerous_patterns.description: The flagged description line contains debugging guidance text and no cryptographic algorithm reference. I did not find evidence of weak cryptography.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is front matter text about root cause investigation. It does not mention MD5, SHA-1, encryption, hashing, keys, or any crypto implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Is Debug Context
dangerous_patterns.description: The flagged question asks what changed before a bug started. This is normal debugging context and not host reconnaissance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line appears inside root cause investigation instructions after git history examples. It does not request system inventory, secrets, environment variables, or network information.

content.user_title: Debug Bugs With Root Cause Discipline
content.value_statement: Bug fixes often fail when teams guess before they understand the failure. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured investigation workflow before implementation.
actual_capabilities: Enforces a four-phase debugging process from investigation to implementation.
Requires stable reproduction details before proposing a fix.
Guides comparison between broken code and working examples.
Prompts the agent to form and test one hypothesis at a time.
Connects implementation to a failing test and verification checklist.
Provides a reusable bug analysis report structure.
limitations: It is a methodology document, not an automated debugger.
It does not include scripts, tools, or integrations.
It depends on available logs, tests, and reproduction steps.
It may slow very small fixes because it favors evidence first.

use_cases.title: Investigate Production Defects
use_cases.description: Use the phased checklist to capture symptoms, reproduction steps, recent changes, and a confirmed root cause before patching.

use_cases.title: Reduce Regression Risk
use_cases.description: Require a failing test and targeted fix before marking a bug resolved.

use_cases.title: Coach Junior Developers
use_cases.description: Turn debugging sessions into a repeatable process with clear stopping points and evidence requirements.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Bug Investigation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use flow-debugging to investigate this bug. Start with the full error details, reproduction steps, and recent changes. Do not suggest a fix yet.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Working and Broken Paths
prompt_templates.prompt: Use flow-debugging Phase 2. Find similar working behavior, compare it with the broken path, and refine the root cause hypothesis.

prompt_templates.title: Test One Hypothesis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use flow-debugging Phase 3. State one testable hypothesis, define the smallest validation step, and record the observed result before any fix.

prompt_templates.title: Implement With Verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Use flow-debugging Phase 4. Write or identify the failing test, implement the smallest root cause fix, and verify the original issue plus regressions.

output_examples.input: A payment callback fails only after deployment.
output: A phase-by-phase bug analysis with error details, reproduction status, environment notes, and recent change review.
A single root cause hypothesis tied to observed evidence.
A verification checklist covering the failing scenario and related regression tests.

output_examples.input: A UI state bug appears after a refactor.
output: A comparison of the broken flow against a working component.
A focused hypothesis about where state diverges.
A minimal implementation plan after the hypothesis is tested.

output_examples.input: Three attempted fixes did not resolve an API error.
output: A stop point that resets the process to root cause investigation.
A list of assumptions that need evidence.
A recommendation to inspect architecture or data flow before another fix.

best_practices: Collect the complete error, stack trace, reproduction steps, and impact before changing code.
Test one hypothesis at a time and record the observed result.
Tie the final fix to a failing test and a clear verification checklist.
anti_patterns: Applying quick fixes before confirming the root cause.
Changing several unrelated files to resolve one bug.
Marking an issue fixed without a reproducing test or manual verification.

faq.question: Does this skill run debugging commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a guidance document. It may suggest local read-only git commands as part of investigation.

faq.question: Can it fix code by itself?
faq.answer: No. It structures the debugging process and delays implementation until the root cause is supported by evidence.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it when a bug has unclear causes, repeated failed fixes, or high regression risk.

faq.question: Does it require test-driven development?
faq.answer: It recommends a failing test before the final fix when a test is practical.

faq.question: Is it useful for small bugs?
faq.answer: Yes, but the full process may be more detailed than needed for simple, obvious issues.

faq.question: What tools does it support?
faq.answer: It is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code because it is plain Markdown guidance.
